transcript: ðŸŽµ Okay, we've been talking about the classification process. And in fact, we just completed looking at the life cycle of assets, for example, and how we need to classify them. right at the beginning so that we can start protecting assets right from the start and as they go through each of their phases of their life cycle. In this section, in this module, we're going to talk about how any asset needs to be protected from two perspectives. So if you think of data, for example, we'll use data as an example of an asset that needs to be protected, and therefore we can use data classification to be able to protect it. We need to classify that data asset. The owners will do that, of course, because they're always accountable for protecting that value of the data. But once it's been classified, any piece of data really can be handled and processed and viewed in two different types of environments. Like for example, we people, maybe using that data as part of a process. But at some point, that data is going to be handled by a system or an architecture, like for example, maybe stored in a database environment. And while people are doing part of some process, they're going to be looking at that data as part of that process. And so protecting data needs to be done from two different perspectives. That's what these words really imply here and will show us the difference between. All right, so if you look at a lot of the guidance that exists out there for data classification systems and so on, you're gonna see that these words that sometimes we use interchangeably don't mean the same thing. And it's important for us to understand the difference because again, that may be reflected in the exam all right so apparently classification and categorization are not the same thing but it highlights the difference of how we can protect data items in whatever state they're being used in so for example classification is the system of classes That's the system itself that allows us to be able to protect data based on its value. So think of classification as the entire classification system that we use to protect, for example, assets within the organization. Categorization is the actual act of taking your assets and placing them into those classification levels, for example, that you've created, like top secret, secret confidential. So the entire system itself is what we call classification. Categorization is the act of taking assets and placing them into those classification levels for the purpose of protecting, for example, those assets themselves. Now here's where The industry provides guidance, for example. But when we look at the typical classification levels that government and military uses, We're all familiar with that because we've all heard, you know, top secret, secret and confidential, for example. And we typically understand that top secret is your most valuable and then secret would be the next and confidential is below, for example. But in industry, for example, we typically don't use those same classification levels. So your organization may be using classification levels like you see here. These are just meant to be examples, financially sensitive, proprietary, you know, it could be related to, for example, privacy information, we call it PII, or it could be company restricted or whatever. The important point that the exam is going to make is whatever classification levels you are using, people need to understand the value of each of those. And this is where security comes in. Security has to provide security. the education, awareness, the training, the skills, for example, for not only owners to be able to understand what the true value of your classification levels are, but also others within the organization like custodians and users. so that when they see something that has been classified at your classification levels, they not only understand the value that is being obviously aligned with those classification levels, but how to also protect based on those classification levels. So it's important for security to support your entire classification systems. And again, the value of data classification and asset classification is not just in having three or four different classification levels that equate the value, but in the underlying mechanisms and technologies and architectures and security controls that the security function would support that really supports the entire best way to protect all assets of the organization, and that is asset classification systems. Now, here's the difference that I was sort of alluding to earlier, and that is any asset can be in different states at different times, whether it's being stored or processed in a system, in its architecture, or whether it's being used by people. as part of a process. And that is the difference between these words that you see here, right? So labeling is what allows systems and architectures and technologies to be able to understand the classification level of a piece of data, for example, so that it can be protected based on that classification level. So perfect example here, let's say a data item has been classified at the top secret level. The system needs to be able to understand that so that it can apply the proper security controls that would apply to top secret. So labeling would allow the system, the technology itself, the application, for example, to understand the classification level of a piece of data so that it can be protected properly. So labeling allows the system to understand the classification level so it can apply the proper security controls. Marking is what allows people that are using that data as part of a process to understand the classification level so that again, we can protect that data item properly based on its marking. All right. So if you remember this, labeling allows the system to understand the classification level. Marking allows people to understand the classification level so that ultimately we can protect data items properly as they're being used, protected, stored, processed, whatever. All right. And so examples of labeling that we talked about before, and of course these, you know, highlight the military, the government ones that are typically used, top secret, secret, public, and so on. But it could be others that you're using within the organization itself, right? Now, how does the system understand this? Because it's the system and the architecture that uses labeling in order to be able to protect that data properly. Well, what we can use are things like watermarks and metadata, for example, which is referred to as data that describes the data. So that could include, for example, its classification level. digital signatures and electronic labels and all kinds of other things that the system itself can use in order to understand the classification levels of that data so it can apply the proper security controls to protect it at that level. Thank you.
